description:
You are acting as an observability engineer who must make sure no-one ships a broken Grafana dashboard
    to production.  
    The staging area already contains four JSON dashboard files:

        /home/user/dashboards/staging/app_latency.json
        /home/user/dashboards/staging/error_rate.json
        /home/user/dashboards/staging/infra_overview.json
        /home/user/dashboards/staging/business_kpis.json

    A checksum manifest has also been supplied:

        /home/user/dashboards/manifest.sha256

    A second directory holds pristine copies of every dashboard:

        /home/user/dashboards/backups/….

    One of the staging dashboards has been corrupted on purpose.  
    Your job is to:

    1. Verify every SHA-256 checksum in /home/user/dashboards/manifest.sha256 against
       the files in /home/user/dashboards/staging.  
       • Treat a file as “pass” when its computed SHA-256 hex digest is **identical**
         to the digest recorded in the manifest line that names the file.  
       • Treat it as “fail” otherwise.  

    2. For every failure, restore the file by copying its counterpart from
       /home/user/dashboards/backups into /home/user/dashboards/staging **overwriting the bad file**.  

    3. Re-run the checksum verification so that, in the end, every dashboard passes.  

    4. Create an audit log called
          /home/user/dashboards/integrity_audit.log
       that captures your whole workflow.  
       The file **must** be plain UTF-8 text and follow the exact CSV layout shown below
       so the automated grader can parse it unambiguously.

    5. Do **not** remove the manifest or the backup directory.

    ----------  REQUIRED LOG FORMAT  ----------
    • First line is a fixed header exactly as written here  
      (no spaces before/after the commas, all lowercase):

        timestamp,operation,filename,expected_sha256,actual_sha256

    • Every subsequent line must contain **five** comma-separated fields:

        1. An ISO-8601 timestamp in UTC to the second  
           (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ, e.g. 2024-03-15T11:28:05Z)  
        2. operation – one of: VERIFY_PASS, VERIFY_FAIL, RESTORE, VERIFY_PASS_FINAL  
        3. filename  – the simple file name, e.g. error_rate.json  
        4. expected_sha256 – the digest exactly as it appears in manifest.sha256  
        5. actual_sha256   – the digest you calculated for the file

      Example row (do **not** reuse the values):

        2024-03-15T11:28:05Z,VERIFY_FAIL,app_latency.json,e3b0c44…,ca97811…

    • After all per-file rows, append **five** statistical summary rows
      (still comma-delimited, two columns only, no header):

        TOTAL_FILES,4
        PASS_INITIAL,3
        FAIL_INITIAL,1
        RESTORED,1
        PASS_FINAL,4

    • The grader will insist on:  
      – the header being present and exact  
      – at least one VERIFY_FAIL and one RESTORE line for app_latency.json  
      – four VERIFY_PASS_FINAL lines (one for each dashboard)  
      – the final statistics matching the numbers above.

    Hints (you are free to choose your own commands):
      • sha256sum is available.  
      • To extract just the checksum value or filename, cut/awk may help.  
      • Remember that the manifest uses two blanks between the digest
        and the file name – exactly what sha256sum outputs by default.
      • Keep your timestamps in UTC; the container’s date command is UTC already.

    When you are finished, the automated tests will:
      • Ensure every dashboard under /home/user/dashboards/staging has the correct
        SHA-256 digest from the manifest.  
      • Read /home/user/dashboards/integrity_audit.log and validate its
        structure and contents against the rules above.

    You do NOT need root privileges for any part of this task.
truth:
<!--
        Pre-existing filesystem layout and contents
        ===========================================

        /home/user/dashboards
        ├── backups
        │   ├── app_latency.json       (good copy, empty file)
        │   ├── error_rate.json        (good copy, empty file)
        │   ├── infra_overview.json    (good copy, empty file)
        │   └── business_kpis.json     (good copy, empty file)
        ├── staging
        │   ├── app_latency.json       (CORRUPTED – single byte "a")
        │   ├── error_rate.json        (good, empty file)
        │   ├── infra_overview.json    (good, empty file)
        │   └── business_kpis.json     (good, empty file)
        └── manifest.sha256

        Permissions are 0644 for files and 0755 for the directories,
        owned by uid/gid of the normal “user” account.

        Check-sum reference values
        --------------------------
        We use two SHA-256 digests:

          EMPTY_FILE_SHA   = e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855
          SINGLE_A_SHA     = ca978112ca1bbdcafac231b39a23dc4da786eff8147c4e72b9807785afee48bb

        • All “good” JSON files (they are completely empty, 0-byte size)
          therefore hash to EMPTY_FILE_SHA.

        • The corrupted staging/app_latency.json contains the single byte
          0x61 ("a") and therefore hashes to SINGLE_A_SHA.

        Contents of individual files
        ----------------------------

        /home/user/dashboards/backups/app_latency.json
        /home/user/dashboards/backups/error_rate.json
        /home/user/dashboards/backups/infra_overview.json
        /home/user/dashboards/backups/business_kpis.json
            # All four files are **completely** empty (size 0 bytes).

        /home/user/dashboards/staging/error_rate.json
        /home/user/dashboards/staging/infra_overview.json
        /home/user/dashboards/staging/business_kpis.json
            # These three are also empty (size 0 bytes).

        /home/user/dashboards/staging/app_latency.json
            # One single character, ASCII “a”, no newline (size 1 byte).

        /home/user/dashboards/manifest.sha256   (exact literal content)
            e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  app_latency.json
            e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  error_rate.json
            e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  infra_overview.json
            e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855  business_kpis.json

        Expected post-task state
        ------------------------
        • staging and backups directories remain.  
        • Every file in /home/user/dashboards/staging must match the
          digest e3b0c44… (EMPTY_FILE_SHA).  
        • A new file /home/user/dashboards/integrity_audit.log exists and
          obeys the strict CSV format laid out in the <task> section.

        The automated test harness will:
          1. compute sha256sum on each staging dashboard and compare to manifest.  
          2. parse integrity_audit.log, enforcing:
              – correct header  
              – at least one VERIFY_FAIL for app_latency.json  
              – one RESTORE for app_latency.json  
              – four VERIFY_PASS_FINAL lines  
              – summary values EXACTLY:
                    TOTAL_FILES,4
                    PASS_INITIAL,3
                    FAIL_INITIAL,1
                    RESTORED,1
                    PASS_FINAL,4
    -->
name: task_000000_bd0c17a2
